name: Cloud Build for CI/CD Automation
description: Building and testing applications using Cloud Build with cloudbuild.yaml, parallel steps, build triggers, and integration with source repositories.
key_facts: Cloud Build executes builds in Docker containers; each step uses a builder image from Artifact Registry or Docker Hub
waitFor: ['-'] runs a step in parallel with all previously defined steps; waitFor: ['step-id'] creates dependencies
Build triggers connect to Cloud Source Repositories, GitHub, and Bitbucket; trigger on push, PR, or tag events
Cloud Build service account needs permissions to push to Artifact Registry and deploy to GKE/Cloud Run
Build substitutions ($BRANCH_NAME, $COMMIT_SHA, $BUILD_ID) inject runtime values into build steps
common_misconceptions: Cloud Build is only for containerized applications — it can build any artifact (JARs, Python packages) by using appropriate builder images
Cloud Build steps must run sequentially — steps can run in parallel using waitFor: ['-'] for faster pipeline execution

name: Cloud Deploy Continuous Delivery Pipelines
description: Managing release pipelines with Cloud Deploy: creating delivery pipelines, promotion sequences, approval gates, and rollback for GKE and Cloud Run targets.
key_facts: Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline defines an ordered sequence of targets (dev -> staging -> prod) for progressive delivery
Releases are immutable; once a release is created with a container image, the same image is promoted through all stages
Approval gates require human approval before promoting a release to specific targets (e.g., production)
Automated rollback triggers can revert a deployment if Cloud Monitoring alerting policies detect degradation
Skaffold manifests define how to render and apply Kubernetes or Cloud Run configurations per target environment
common_misconceptions: Cloud Deploy automatically runs tests before promotion — tests must be configured as separate verification jobs or Cloud Build steps
Rollback in Cloud Deploy creates a new deployment of the previous version — rollback re-deploys the prior release without creating a new one

name: SLIs, SLOs, and Error Budgets
description: Defining meaningful Service Level Indicators and Objectives, calculating error budgets, and using error budget policies to balance reliability with feature velocity.
key_facts: SLI examples: availability (successful requests/total requests), latency (p99 < 200ms), throughput (requests/second)
Error budget = 1 - SLO target; for 99.9% SLO, error budget is 0.1% = ~43.8 minutes/month
When error budget is exhausted, feature deployments stop and the team focuses on reliability improvements
SLO windows: rolling 28-day windows are common; a window that's too short leads to alert fatigue
Cloud Monitoring SLO monitoring creates burn rate alerts (fast burn = high urgency, slow burn = ticket-level urgency)
common_misconceptions: SLOs should always be 99.999% — 'five nines' is expensive; choose SLOs based on actual user expectations and business impact
Exceeding the SLO target is always good — spending error budget means less reliability margin; aim for target, not above

name: Cloud Monitoring Dashboards and Alerting
description: Creating custom monitoring dashboards, configuring alerting policies with notification channels, and managing uptime checks for service health.
key_facts: Alert policies define conditions (metric threshold, uptime check failure), notification channels (email, PagerDuty, Pub/Sub), and incident creation
Custom metrics from Cloud Logging log-based metrics and application-emitted metrics via Cloud Monitoring API
Uptime checks test URL, TCP port, or DNS endpoints from 6 global regions; use for external health monitoring
Monitoring query language (MQL) enables complex metric queries including aggregation, ratio, and time-shift operations
Dashboards and alerts can be managed as code using Terraform provider for google_monitoring_alert_policy
common_misconceptions: Alerts should be set for every metric — alert only on actionable signals; too many alerts cause alert fatigue and ignored notifications
Cloud Monitoring only works with GCP resources — it monitors on-premises and multi-cloud resources with the Ops Agent

name: Cloud Logging Structured Logging and Log Management
description: Configuring structured logging, log-based metrics, log sinks, and log analytics for operational visibility and long-term log retention.
key_facts: Structured JSON logs parsed by Cloud Logging enable filtering on log fields (e.g., severity, httpRequest.status, labels)
Log sinks export to Cloud Storage (archive), BigQuery (analysis), Pub/Sub (real-time SIEM streaming), or Log Buckets
Log-based metrics create custom Prometheus-style metrics from log entries for dashboards and alerting
Log exclusion filters reduce logging costs by dropping high-volume, low-value logs before ingestion
Log Analytics (with Upgrade) uses BigQuery to analyze logs with SQL for historical investigations
common_misconceptions: All logs are retained forever by default — Cloud Logging retains most logs for 30 days; use sinks to Cloud Storage for longer retention
Cloud Logging handles log ingestion automatically for all workloads — VMs require the Ops Agent to stream application and system logs

name: Incident Management and Postmortems
description: Managing production incidents using structured response procedures, blameless postmortems, and action items to improve system reliability.
key_facts: Incident severity levels (P1-P4) define response times and escalation paths; P1 is complete service outage
Incident commander role coordinates response; separates diagnosis from communication to avoid cross-talk
Blameless postmortems focus on system improvements, not individual blame; use 5-Whys for root cause analysis
MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) and MTTD (Mean Time To Detect) are key SRE reliability metrics
Error budget burn rate alerts (2% burn in 1 hour or 5% in 6 hours) provide early warning before SLO violation
common_misconceptions: Postmortems are only needed for major outages — even minor incidents provide learning opportunities; document all P1-P3 incidents
Fast MTTR means the team is good at firefighting — reducing MTTD and improving automation reduces the need for firefighting

name: GKE Operations for DevOps Engineers
description: Managing GKE cluster operations including upgrades, multi-cluster deployments, workload identity, and operational tooling for DevOps teams.
key_facts: GKE release channels: Rapid (latest), Regular (default), Stable; auto-upgrade maintains versions within chosen channel
Config Sync and Policy Controller (Anthos Config Management) enforce GitOps patterns for Kubernetes configuration
GKE Workload Identity is the recommended method for granting GKE pods access to GCP APIs; eliminates key files
GKE Gateway API (successor to Ingress) provides richer traffic management for multi-cluster deployments
GKE multi-cluster Ingress (MCI) routes global traffic to the nearest healthy cluster using Google's global LB
common_misconceptions: GKE Autopilot requires no operational effort — you still need to manage workloads, RBAC, and networking; Autopilot only manages nodes
GKE auto-upgrade always happens immediately — auto-upgrades follow the release channel cadence and maintenance windows

name: Terraform and Infrastructure as Code for GCP
description: Managing GCP infrastructure with Terraform using remote state in Cloud Storage, modules, and CI/CD pipeline integration for automated infrastructure provisioning.
key_facts: Terraform state stored in Cloud Storage with state locking via Cloud Firestore or GCS native locking prevents concurrent modifications
terraform plan shows proposed changes; terraform apply executes them; terraform destroy tears down infrastructure
GCP Cloud Foundation Toolkit provides opinionated Terraform modules for org structure, VPC, GKE, and GCR
Atlantis or terraform-cloud integrates Terraform with GitHub PRs for automated plan and apply in CI/CD
Workspaces in Terraform separate state per environment (dev/staging/prod) using the same configuration
common_misconceptions: terraform apply is always safe to run — always review terraform plan output before applying; misconfigurations can delete production resources
IaC eliminates all configuration drift — runtime changes outside Terraform cause drift; use terraform plan regularly to detect and remediate

name: Google Cloud Organization Bootstrap and Landing Zone
description: Setting up a GCP organization with folder hierarchy, shared VPC, centralized logging, billing controls, and organization policies using the Cloud Foundation Fabric.
key_facts: Landing zone components: org structure (folders/projects), networking (Shared VPC), IAM (groups/roles), logging, billing controls
Organization bootstrap project (seed project) deploys Terraform using org admin permissions to create the landing zone
Google Cloud Setup Checklist and Cloud Foundation Toolkit provide reference implementations for enterprise LZ
Centralized logging: all-projects log sinks route to a central logging project with BigQuery for long-term analysis
Cloud Identity or Google Workspace is required for the domain-based org; GCDS syncs on-premises AD to Cloud Identity
common_misconceptions: A GCP organization can be set up without Cloud Identity — an org node requires a Cloud Identity or Workspace domain
All projects must be under folders — projects can exist directly under the organization; folders add optional grouping

name: Distributed Tracing and Profiling
description: Implementing distributed tracing with Cloud Trace and continuous profiling with Cloud Profiler to diagnose latency and CPU performance issues.
key_facts: Cloud Trace automatically collects traces for App Engine, Cloud Run, and GKE with no instrumentation for HTTP requests
OpenTelemetry SDK provides vendor-neutral tracing instrumentation; traces export to Cloud Trace, Jaeger, or Zipkin
Trace sampling rate balances visibility with overhead; 100% sampling is feasible for low-traffic services
Cloud Profiler uses statistical sampling for CPU and heap profiling with less than 1% performance overhead
Trace context propagation (W3C TraceContext headers) links spans across microservices for end-to-end visibility
common_misconceptions: Distributed tracing requires rewriting all services — OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation handles common frameworks without code changes
High latency in traces is always a database query issue — traces reveal all bottlenecks including serialization, lock contention, and network

name: Chaos Engineering and Reliability Testing
description: Implementing chaos engineering practices on GCP to validate system resilience through controlled failure injection and game day exercises.
key_facts: Chaos engineering hypothesis: 'the system will exhibit normal behavior when X fails'; run controlled experiments to verify
GCP reliability test tools: simulate zone failure by stopping a MIG in one zone, test load balancer failover by terminating backends
Game days simulate disaster scenarios (region failure, network partition) with recovery objectives measured against SLOs
Cloud Scheduler can trigger synthetic load or failure injection functions for scheduled reliability tests
Gradual rollouts (Cloud Deploy traffic splitting) reduce blast radius during production changes
common_misconceptions: Chaos engineering means randomly breaking production systems — chaos engineering is controlled experiments with clear hypotheses and abort conditions
Chaos engineering is only for Netflix-scale companies — any production system benefits from controlled failure testing

name: Artifact Registry for Software Artifact Management
description: Managing Docker images, Helm charts, Maven, npm, and Python packages in Artifact Registry with vulnerability scanning and access control.
key_facts: Artifact Registry supports Docker, Helm, Maven, npm, Python (pip), Apt, and Yum package formats in one service
Container Analysis automatically scans Docker images for CVEs using OS and language package vulnerability databases
Remote repositories proxy public registries (Docker Hub, Maven Central) with caching for improved reliability
Virtual repositories aggregate multiple repositories behind a single endpoint for flexible package resolution
IAM roles control access: artifactregistry.reader (pull), artifactregistry.writer (push), artifactregistry.admin
common_misconceptions: Container Registry and Artifact Registry are the same — Artifact Registry supersedes Container Registry with multi-format support and better IAM
Public Docker Hub images don't need scanning — images from public registries should always be scanned before use in production

name: Cost Management and FinOps for DevOps
description: Implementing FinOps practices to optimize GCP costs in CI/CD pipelines, container workloads, and monitoring systems.
key_facts: Cloud Build pricing: first 120 minutes/day free; costs scale with machine type and duration of build steps
GKE cost optimization: Spot VMs for CI/CD workers, node auto-provisioning for right-sized node pools, idle cluster management
Budget alerts with Pub/Sub notifications trigger automated cost governance actions (e.g., stop dev VMs at night)
Cloud Billing export to BigQuery enables detailed cost analysis by service, SKU, project, and label
Recommenders (Idle VM, Unattended Project, Cost) continuously suggest cost savings based on usage patterns
common_misconceptions: Cost optimization is only the platform team's responsibility — DevOps engineers influence costs through build efficiency, resource sizing, and cleanup automation
Labels automatically reduce costs — labels only enable cost attribution; cost reduction requires action based on the insights

name: DevSecOps and Secure CI/CD Pipelines
description: Integrating security into CI/CD pipelines with Binary Authorization, container scanning, SAST, DAST, and supply chain security controls.
key_facts: SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) defines provenance requirements; Google Cloud DSSE attestations support SLSA Level 3
Binary Authorization enforces deploy-time policy: require attestations from security scanning, QA approval, and code review sign-offs
Cloud Build can run SAST tools (SonarQube, Semgrep) and DAST tools (OWASP ZAP) as build steps in the pipeline
Secrets in Cloud Build: use Cloud Secret Manager version references in cloudbuild.yaml; never hardcode in YAML
Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated by Artifact Registry helps track vulnerable dependencies across services
common_misconceptions: Security scanning in CI/CD slows down pipelines too much — most scanning tools take under 5 minutes; this is acceptable for production protection
DevSecOps means the security team runs CI/CD — DevSecOps integrates security tooling; developers own fixing the findings

name: Service Performance Optimization
description: Optimizing GKE and Cloud Run service performance using VPA, HPA, KEDA, load testing, and performance benchmarking on Google Cloud.
key_facts: GKE Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) recommends and sets right-sized CPU/memory requests based on observed usage
KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) scales deployments based on Pub/Sub queue depth, Bigtable, or custom metrics
Load testing with Locust or k6 on Cloud Run validates system behavior under expected and peak load conditions
Cloud Trace identifies tail latency bottlenecks; Cloud Profiler finds CPU hotspots in application code
GKE Node Auto Provisioning creates right-sized node pools automatically based on pending pod resource requests
common_misconceptions: Setting resource limits too high is a safe default — oversized limits waste cluster capacity and increase cost; right-size with VPA recommendations
Load testing in staging is sufficient — production traffic patterns often differ; use gradual rollouts to test under real conditions
